title: Short-lived feature branches

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Short-lived feature branches; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Pro Git; second edition online; section applicable to Short-lived feature branches
sources.claim: A branch workflow isolates a coherent change, starts from an intentional base, incorporates reviewable commits, and synchronizes with the target branch before integration.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Engineering; August 2023; section applicable to Short-lived feature branches
sources.claim: A branch workflow isolates a coherent change, starts from an intentional base, incorporates reviewable commits, and synchronizes with the target branch before integration.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A branch workflow isolates a coherent change, starts from an intentional base, incorporates reviewable commits, and synchronizes with the target branch before integration.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A short feature branch begins from current main, contains one tested concern, and opens a review before merging back. Your goal is to complete an isolated feature branch and verify the base and tip before integration. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Commits and history; Branches as movable refs; Commit messages and scope. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A branch workflow isolates a coherent change, starts from an intentional base, incorporates reviewable commits, and synchronizes with the target branch before integration. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Long-lived divergence, stacked branches, release branches, and protected targets require project-specific update and integration rules. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Creating many branches automatically prevents conflicts and guarantees changes are based on the latest target.

includes: Create, switch, update and delete branches around one coherent change.
Complete an isolated feature branch and verify the base and tip before integration.
excludes: Long-lived divergence, stacked branches, release branches, and protected targets require project-specific update and integration rules.

outcomes: Complete an isolated feature branch and verify the base and tip before integration.

examples.title: Short-lived feature branches: direct case
examples.situation: A short feature branch begins from current main, contains one tested concern, and opens a review before merging back.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A short feature branch begins from current main, contains one tested concern, and opens a review before merging back.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Branches provide named isolation, but developers must choose the base, keep scope coherent, update deliberately, test, and integrate through the agreed process.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Branches provide named isolation, but developers must choose the base, keep scope coherent, update deliberately, test, and integrate through the agreed process.

examples.title: Short-lived feature branches: boundary case
examples.situation: Before new work, the learner verifies the active branch and clean status so changes do not accidentally land on main.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Before new work, the learner verifies the active branch and clean status so changes do not accidentally land on main.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Long-lived divergence, stacked branches, release branches, and protected targets require project-specific update and integration rules.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Creating many branches automatically prevents conflicts and guarantees changes are based on the latest target.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Complete an isolated feature branch and verify the base and tip before integration.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Creating many branches automatically prevents conflicts and guarantees changes are based on the latest target.
misconceptions.correction: Branches provide named isolation, but developers must choose the base, keep scope coherent, update deliberately, test, and integrate through the agreed process.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Creating many branches automatically prevents conflicts and guarantees changes are based on the latest target.

analogy.example: A route map can model Short-lived feature branches: movable signposts identify current destinations, routes diverge, and integration reconnects selected paths.
usefulFor: Visualizing references, divergence, ancestry, merge bases, and reconciled paths
limitations: Branches are lightweight references to commits, not copied roads or independent containers of files.
Long-lived divergence, stacked branches, release branches, and protected targets require project-specific update and integration rules.

recap.summary: You should now be able to complete an isolated feature branch and verify the base and tip before integration. Keep this boundary in view: Long-lived divergence, stacked branches, release branches, and protected targets require project-specific update and integration rules.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Short-lived feature branches without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Creating many branches automatically prevents conflicts and guarantees changes are based on the latest target.
Apply the model to this case from memory: Before new work, the learner verifies the active branch and clean status so changes do not accidentally land on main.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Fast-forward and three-way merge”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
